skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation skill for AI agents using the inference.sh CLI. It opens web pages, snapshots elements as @e refs, clicks, fills forms, uploads files, runs page JavaScript, captures screenshots, records video, and supports proxies.
skill.summary: Control browser sessions through inference.sh with element refs, screenshots, video, JavaScript execution, forms, uploads, and proxy support.
skill.author: skillssh

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly true as documented shell examples and templates, but they are expected for an infsh-based browser automation skill. The higher risk is semantic: the skill can send credentials, cookies, screenshots, videos, uploaded files, proxy details, and arbitrary page JavaScript tasks through a hosted browser service. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than critical.

high_findings.title: Remote Browser Automation Handles Sensitive Sessions
high_findings.description: The skill is explicitly allowed to run infsh commands and documents login, OAuth, 2FA, session reuse, screenshots, and video. This can expose credentials, authenticated page content, cookies, and recordings to a hosted browser workflow if used on sensitive accounts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability is directly documented and the security notes acknowledge sensitive video exposure. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, but the data exposure risk is clear.

high_findings.title: Cookie Extraction Pattern Documented
high_findings.description: The authentication reference includes a cookie extraction section that runs page JavaScript to read document.cookie and then prints the cookies. This can expose session tokens or other sensitive cookie values in logs or downstream outputs.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The finding is based on a concrete documented example that extracts and echoes cookies. The exact sensitivity depends on the target site and cookie flags.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: The command reference exposes an execute function that runs caller-supplied JavaScript in the page and returns results and screenshots. This is a powerful browser capability that can extract page content or trigger actions beyond the element-ref API.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execute function and examples are explicit. The risk is high when used on authenticated or sensitive pages, but legitimate automation uses also exist.

high_findings.title: Proxy Support Enables Anonymized or Evasive Browsing
high_findings.description: The proxy documentation supports HTTP, HTTPS, SOCKS5, authenticated proxies, privacy-focused routing, and proxy rotation for scraping. These features are legitimate for testing but can also support abuse or policy evasion.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The feature is intentional and documented, including rate-limit avoidance language. This is dual-use rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Templates Execute User-Supplied URLs and Write Artifacts
medium_findings.description: The templates are bash scripts that pass caller-supplied URLs to infsh and write screenshots, text, links, and video metadata into an output directory. This is expected behavior, but it increases operational risk if run on untrusted targets or sensitive pages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell behavior and file writes are directly visible in the template. The command injection evidence is limited because arguments are mostly quoted, so the concern is operational exposure.

medium_findings.title: File Upload Paths Can Expose Local Files
medium_findings.description: The skill documents an upload action that accepts file_paths and sends selected files through the browser automation flow. This can disclose local files if an agent is instructed to upload sensitive paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload capability is clearly documented. Risk depends on user approval and which file paths are supplied.

medium_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Findings Are Mostly Expected
medium_findings.description: The many command-substitution and shell-invocation findings are real syntax in documentation and shell templates, but they primarily wrap infsh, jq, grep, curl, and file output for the advertised browser automation workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns exist, but their context is examples and templates rather than hidden payloads. Some user-controlled values are passed to commands, so the finding is not fully dismissible.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most hardcoded URL findings point to example.com, documentation links, httpbin, or placeholder proxy domains. These are normal examples, not evidence of data exfiltration to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are examples or product documentation. I did not find a suspicious exfiltration endpoint in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Links and Text Trigger Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Several path traversal and weak cryptography alerts appear to come from Markdown documentation text, such as relative links and ordinary reference content. I did not find evidence that these lines implement path traversal or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation metadata or relative Markdown links, not executable filesystem or cryptographic logic. Some static findings in shell templates remain separately evaluated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Allowlist for infsh Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands, enabling the agent to invoke the hosted browser automation service directly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is direct evidence. The risk is inherent to the skill design.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screenshot and Video Capture
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can capture screenshots and session videos, including full-page captures and cursor-visible recordings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capture capability is documented in the function table and video reference. Sensitive exposure depends on page content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Proxy Secret Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include password fields, proxy usernames, proxy passwords, and environment-sourced credentials passed into browser sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret-handling paths are explicit in the documentation. The skill also recommends environment variables, which reduces but does not remove risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary URL Navigation
dangerous_patterns.description: The open and goto actions accept URLs, allowing the browser session to navigate to arbitrary targets selected by the caller.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The open input and goto action are documented. This is expected for browser automation but expands network reach.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows with Agent Browser
content.value_statement: AI agents need a compact way to inspect and operate web pages. This skill provides browser sessions, element references, screenshots, videos, form actions, uploads, proxies, and JavaScript execution through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Opens browser sessions for supplied URLs through the inference.sh CLI.
Returns compact @e element references for clicks, fills, selects, hovers, drags, scrolling, and navigation.
Captures viewport or full-page screenshots and can record a session video until close.
Runs caller-supplied JavaScript in the current page and returns the result.
Supports file upload inputs and proxy configuration, including authenticated proxies.
Includes shell templates for form automation, authenticated sessions, and page content capture.
limitations: It requires the infsh CLI and access to the hosted inference.sh browser service.
Element refs can become stale after navigation or dynamic page updates.
Screenshots, videos, cookies, form data, and proxy credentials may involve sensitive data.
It does not add authorization checks for target sites or submitted automation tasks.

use_cases.title: Automate QA Browser Checks
use_cases.description: Run repeatable browser flows, capture screenshots, and record videos for debugging failed UI interactions.

use_cases.title: Collect Web Research Evidence
use_cases.description: Open public pages, extract visible text and links, and save screenshots for later review.

use_cases.title: Prototype Agentic Web Tasks
use_cases.description: Test how an agent can navigate pages using compact element refs before building a dedicated integration.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Summarize a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this URL with agent-browser, list the visible interactive elements, take a screenshot, and summarize what the page is for: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Test Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to inspect this form, identify the fields, fill them with safe test data, submit it, and report the final page state: [FORM_URL].

prompt_templates.title: Capture Research Artifacts
prompt_templates.prompt: Visit these public pages with agent-browser, capture full-page screenshots, extract visible text and links, and organize the results by source: [URL_LIST].

prompt_templates.title: Debug an Authenticated Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to run this approved test workflow with recording enabled, stop before entering real secrets, and report stale refs, failed actions, and screenshots: [WORKFLOW].

output_examples.input: Open a public product page and capture its main interactive elements.
output: Opened the page and returned the current title and URL.
Identified navigation links, search input, product options, and primary action buttons as element refs.
Captured a viewport screenshot for visual confirmation.

output_examples.input: Record a failed checkout test using test credentials only.
output: Started a recorded browser session and followed the requested checkout steps.
Detected a failed click caused by a stale element ref after navigation.
Returned the final page state, screenshot, and video reference for debugging.

output_examples.input: Extract text and links from a documentation page.
output: Captured page text, page links, and a full-page screenshot.
Saved the extracted content into separate review artifacts.
Closed the browser session after collection completed.

best_practices: Use only approved URLs and test accounts when automating authenticated pages.
Disable video recording and screenshots when pages may show secrets or private user data.
Re-snapshot after navigation, form submission, or dynamic content changes before using refs again.
anti_patterns: Do not enter production credentials into remote browser sessions without a formal data handling review.
Do not use proxy rotation to bypass rate limits, access controls, or site terms.
Do not run arbitrary JavaScript on pages containing sensitive customer or account data.

faq.question: What does this skill need to run?
faq.answer: It needs the inference.sh CLI and permission to run infsh commands from the AI tool.

faq.question: Does it browse locally or through a service?
faq.answer: The documentation describes browser automation through inference.sh, with Playwright used under the hood.

faq.question: Can it handle login flows?
faq.answer: Yes, the references and templates show login, OAuth, 2FA, and authenticated session patterns.

faq.question: Can it record videos?
faq.answer: Yes, recording can be enabled when a session opens, and the video is returned when the session closes.

faq.question: Can it upload files?
faq.answer: Yes, the interact action supports upload through supplied file paths.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: It can process credentials, cookies, screenshots, video, uploaded files, proxy credentials, and page JavaScript through a remote browser workflow.
